enrichment.short_description: Roboflow is an end-to-end computer vision platform for annotating images, training models, and deploying them to the cloud or edge devices.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription (per-workspace credits, plus custom Enterprise pricing)
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Public plan); Core plan from $79/month billed annually
enrichment.headquarters: Des Moines, Iowa, United States (with a San Francisco presence)
enrichment.seo_title: Roboflow Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Roboflow costs, who it is for, and how its computer vision annotation, training, and deployment tools compare before you sign up.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Roboflow is a computer vision platform founded in 2019 by Brad Dwyer and Joseph Nelson, who started the company after building an augmented-reality Sudoku-solving demo and later joined Y Combinator's Summer 2020 batch. The company is headquartered in Des Moines, Iowa, with an additional presence in San Francisco, and reported figures put its team size in roughly the 100 to 130 employee range as of the mid-2020s, according to sources such as PitchBook and Tracxn. Roboflow's product covers the full computer vision development lifecycle: image and video dataset upload, collaborative annotation tools with AI-assisted labeling, dataset versioning and augmentation, model training (including its own fast object detection and classification architectures as well as support for popular open-source models such as YOLO variants), and one-click deployment to cloud API endpoints, edge devices, or on-premises hardware through tools like the Roboflow Inference server. It also hosts Roboflow Universe, a public library of shared datasets and pre-trained models that developers can fork and reuse, and Roboflow Workflows, a visual builder for chaining vision models and logic into production pipelines. Pricing is structured as a free Public plan (two users, roughly sixty dollars per month in usage credits, community support, and public datasets only), a Core plan priced at seventy-nine dollars per month when billed annually or ninety-nine dollars per month billed monthly (three base users, private data and models, training analytics, and model evaluation tools), and a custom-quoted Enterprise plan that adds commercial inference licensing, priority GPU access, role-based access control, workflow versioning, and dedicated support. Roboflow states that it serves more than one million developers, including engineers at over half of the Fortune 100, and is commonly used for applications such as manufacturing defect detection, agricultural monitoring, retail shelf analysis, and sports analytics. Because pricing is credit-based and scales with training compute, storage, and inference volume rather than a flat per-seat fee, actual monthly costs can vary significantly depending on usage, and prospective buyers are advised to check Roboflow's official pricing page for current credit allotments before committing to a plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Roboflow is a computer vision platform that helps developers and teams build, train, and deploy image and video recognition models without assembling the entire machine learning pipeline from scratch. Founded in 2019 and headquartered in Des Moines, Iowa, the company grew out of a Y Combinator-backed project and now serves over a million developers worldwide.

The platform spans the full workflow: uploading and annotating datasets, applying preprocessing and augmentation, training custom models or fine-tuning open-source architectures, and deploying the result to the cloud, an API endpoint, or edge hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Roboflow's annotation suite includes AI-assisted labeling tools that speed up bounding-box, polygon, and keypoint annotation, along with dataset versioning so teams can track changes to training data over time. Its Workflows builder lets users chain together multiple models and logic blocks into a single visual pipeline for tasks like counting, classification, and multi-stage detection.

On the deployment side, Roboflow supports cloud-hosted inference APIs, an edge device sandbox for testing on hardware like NVIDIA Jetson or Raspberry Pi, and downloadable model weights for self-managed deployment. Roboflow Universe gives users access to a large public library of community-shared datasets and pre-trained models.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Roboflow offers a free Public plan with two users, around sixty dollars per month in usage credits, and access to public datasets and models only. The Core plan costs seventy-nine dollars per month billed annually (or ninety-nine dollars per month billed monthly), supports three base users and up to twenty projects, and unlocks private data, training analytics, and concurrent model training.

Enterprise pricing is custom and adds commercial inference licensing, priority GPU access, role-based access control, and dedicated support. Because usage is credit-based, actual costs scale with training and inference volume rather than being a fixed per-seat fee.

features.title: Edge and cloud deployment
features.description: Deploy trained models to a hosted cloud API, download weights for self-hosting, or run inference on edge devices via Roboflow Inference.

features.description: Browse and reuse a public library of community-contributed datasets and pre-trained models across many industries.

features.description: Review precision, recall, and confusion matrix data for trained models to compare versions before deploying.

features.description: Apply resizing, cropping, and augmentation transformations to datasets to improve model generalization.

Large public dataset and model library via Roboflow Universe lowers the barrier to getting started
Supports both cloud inference and edge/on-device deployment options
Free tier is usable for small projects without requiring a credit card
cons: Credit-based pricing can make monthly costs unpredictable for compute-heavy workloads
Core plan project and user limits may be restrictive for growing teams
Enterprise features like commercial inference licensing require custom sales conversations
Advanced computer vision concepts still require some ML familiarity to use effectively

faqs.answer: Yes, Roboflow offers a free Public plan that includes annotation tools, model training, and cloud deployment, though projects and datasets on this plan are public.

faqs.answer: The Core plan costs seventy-nine dollars per month when billed annually, or ninety-nine dollars per month when billed monthly.

faqs.answer: Yes, Roboflow supports training and fine-tuning popular open-source architectures alongside its own model options.

enrichment.short_description: RobustIRC is a free, open-source, fault-tolerant IRC network implementation that uses the Raft consensus algorithm to eliminate netsplits.
enrichment.seo_title: Robust IRC Review: Fault-Tolerant Open-Source IRC Server Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what Robust IRC is, how its Raft-based architecture eliminates netsplits, and how to self-host this open-source IRC network software.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RobustIRC is an open-source IRC network implementation created by developer Michael Stapelberg, best known for other open-source projects such as the i3 window manager and Debian Code Search. Unlike traditional IRC daemon software such as ircd or UnrealIRCd, RobustIRC is designed from the ground up as a distributed, fault-tolerant system rather than a single server process linked to other servers in a federation. It is implemented in the Go programming language and uses the Raft consensus algorithm, the same class of algorithm used by systems like etcd and Consul, so that a cluster of RobustIRC nodes can agree on network state and continue operating even if individual nodes fail or are taken down for maintenance. This design directly targets the most notorious reliability problem in traditional IRC networks: netsplits, where a break in server-to-server links causes users on either side to lose sight of each other and can trigger duplicate nick collisions when the split heals. Because RobustIRC nodes share consensus-backed state rather than relying on a fragile mesh of server links, rolling upgrades, machine restarts, and individual node failures can happen without users on the network noticing any disruption in normal use, and client sessions are preserved transparently across brief connectivity interruptions. End users connect through a RobustIRC bridge component that allows standard, off-the-shelf IRC clients to work with the network without needing any special client-side support. The project is fully open source, hosted on GitHub under the RobustIRC organization, and Stapelberg has presented it at multiple developer conferences and community events, including Easterhegg, the Gulaschprogrammiernacht (GPN), Chaos Communication Camp, and GopherCon, where he discussed both the motivation behind the project and its Raft-based implementation in Go. As a niche, community-maintained developer tool rather than a commercial product, RobustIRC has no pricing tiers, company structure, or corporate headquarters; it is distributed for free and intended primarily for technically sophisticated users, IRC network operators, and homelab or self-hosting enthusiasts who want a more resilient alternative to conventional IRC server software. Because it is a smaller, specialized open-source project rather than a funded startup, detailed information about its founding date, contributor count, or adoption metrics is limited compared to commercial SaaS tools, and prospective users are encouraged to consult the project's official documentation and GitHub repository directly for the most current technical details.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RobustIRC is an open-source IRC network implementation built by developer Michael Stapelberg to solve one of IRC's longest-standing reliability problems: netsplits. Instead of linking independent IRC servers together in a traditional federation, RobustIRC runs a cluster of nodes that share consensus-backed state using the Raft algorithm, so the network keeps functioning even when individual nodes fail or restart.

The project is implemented in Go and is fully open source, distributed through GitHub. It is aimed at technically sophisticated users and IRC network operators rather than mainstream chat users, and standard IRC clients connect through a bridge component without needing special support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: RobustIRC's core feature is its Raft-based distributed architecture, which allows multiple server nodes to agree on network state and tolerate node failures without causing netsplits or duplicate nickname collisions. This also enables rolling upgrades and maintenance restarts that are largely invisible to connected users.

The project preserves client sessions transparently across brief connectivity interruptions and works with unmodified, standard IRC clients via a bridge, meaning operators do not need users to adopt any special software to benefit from the added resiliency.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RobustIRC is free and open source with no commercial pricing tiers. It is self-hosted software intended for network operators and developers comfortable running and maintaining their own infrastructure.

features.title: Raft consensus architecture
features.description: Uses the Raft algorithm so a cluster of nodes agrees on network state, avoiding the split-brain problems of traditional IRC server federations.

features.title: Netsplit elimination
features.description: Distributed state sharing prevents the classic IRC netsplit scenario where users on either side of a broken link lose visibility of each other.

features.title: Transparent session preservation
features.description: Client sessions survive brief connectivity interruptions without users needing to manually reconnect or lose their state.

features.title: Rolling upgrades without disruption
features.description: Individual nodes can be restarted or upgraded while the cluster continues serving users, enabling maintenance without visible downtime.

features.title: Standard IRC client compatibility
features.description: A bridge component lets ordinary, unmodified IRC clients connect to a RobustIRC network without special client-side support.

features.title: Written in Go
features.description: Implemented in the Go programming language, making it straightforward to build, deploy, and audit as a single compiled binary.

pros: Eliminates netsplits through a distributed, consensus-based architecture
Free and fully open source with no licensing cost
Compatible with standard, unmodified IRC clients via a bridge
Supports maintenance and upgrades without visible downtime for users
cons: Requires running and maintaining a multi-node cluster, adding operational complexity versus a single IRC daemon
Niche adoption compared to mainstream IRC server software
Limited public information on active development pace and community size
Self-hosted only, with no managed or hosted commercial offering

faqs.answer: It uses the Raft consensus algorithm so multiple server nodes share agreed-upon network state, avoiding the divided views that cause traditional netsplits.

enrichment.short_description: Rocket Matter is a cloud-based legal practice management platform for law firms, covering matter management, billing, and time tracking.
enrichment.pricing_model: Per-user subscription, billed annually or monthly
enrichment.seo_title: Rocket Matter Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare Rocket Matter's legal practice management pricing tiers, features, and pros and cons before choosing law firm software.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rocket Matter is a cloud-based legal practice management software platform built for solo attorneys, small firms, and larger legal practices, founded in 2008 and headquartered in Boca Raton, Florida. The company markets itself as having pioneered cloud-based legal software and has been recognized by outlets such as Forbes for practice management suitability across firm sizes. Rocket Matter's platform centers on matter management, time tracking and billing, trust accounting, and client communication tools designed specifically for the legal industry's compliance and ethics requirements, such as segregated trust account handling. The product is organized into four subscription tiers billed per user per month, with lower pricing available on annual billing plans: Essentials at fifty-nine dollars per user per month provides core matter management plus billing and time tracking, twenty-five gigabytes of document storage, and up to five e-signatures per user per month; Pro at ninety-five dollars per user per month adds unlimited document storage, matter templates and workflows, kanban-style matter boards, client portal access, and fifteen e-signatures per user per month; Premier at one hundred fifteen dollars per user per month layers on advanced billing capabilities such as batch billing, flat fees, and split billing along with more granular user and billing permissions; and Elite at one hundred forty-five dollars per user per month adds built-in legal-specific business accounting, automatic bank data feeds, and trust and financial compliance reporting. All plans include unlimited customer support and free data migration from a firm's previous system. Rocket Matter also offers optional add-ons, including a Core CRM module at thirty-nine dollars per user per month and an Enterprise CRM tier at one hundred forty-seven dollars per month for up to three users, both of which carry a separate three hundred ninety-nine dollar onboarding fee, as well as optional website hosting for one hundred forty-nine dollars per month across all tiers. It is important not to confuse Rocket Matter with the similarly named Rocket.Chat: Rocket Matter is legal practice management software for law firms, while Rocket.Chat is an unrelated open-source team messaging platform, and the two share no corporate ownership, product overlap, or technical relationship despite the similar name prefix. Rocket Matter competes with other legal practice management tools such as Clio and MyCase, and its pricing and feature tiers are positioned to scale from solo practitioners needing basic billing up to larger firms requiring full trust accounting and business financial reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rocket Matter is a cloud-based legal practice management platform founded in 2008 and headquartered in Boca Raton, Florida. It is built specifically for law firms, covering matter management, time tracking, billing, and trust accounting in a single system designed around legal industry compliance needs.
The platform serves solo attorneys through larger firms, with tiered plans that add progressively more advanced billing, accounting, and workflow automation features as firms grow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include matter and case management, time tracking tied directly to billing, document storage, e-signature requests, and a client portal for secure communication and file sharing. Higher tiers add matter templates, kanban-style workflow boards, and advanced billing options such as batch billing, flat fees, and split billing across timekeepers.

At the top tier, Rocket Matter includes built-in legal-specific business accounting with automatic bank data feeds and trust and financial compliance reporting, addressing the strict trust accounting rules that govern how law firms must handle client funds.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rocket Matter is priced per user per month across four tiers billed annually: Essentials at fifty-nine dollars, Pro at ninety-five dollars, Premier at one hundred fifteen dollars, and Elite at one hundred forty-five dollars. All tiers include unlimited support and free data migration from a firm's prior software.

Optional add-ons include a CRM module starting at thirty-nine dollars per user per month plus a three hundred ninety-nine dollar onboarding fee, and website hosting at one hundred forty-nine dollars per month regardless of plan tier.

enrichment.short_description: Rocket.Chat is an open-source team chat and collaboration platform that can be self-hosted or used via managed cloud plans, with a focus on security and data sovereignty.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (free self-hosted Community edition, plus paid Pro and Enterprise plans)
enrichment.headquarters: Wilmington, Delaware, United States (with an R&D office in Porto Alegre, Brazil)
languages: Available in 30+ interface languages via community translation
enrichment.seo_title: Rocket.Chat Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Rocket.Chat's free and paid plans compare, what features are included, and whether this open-source team chat platform fits your needs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rocket.Chat is an open-source team chat and collaboration platform founded in 2015 by Gabriel Engel, built as an alternative to proprietary messaging tools like Slack and Microsoft Teams with an emphasis on self-hosting, data sovereignty, and security-focused deployments. The company behind the product, Rocket.Chat Technologies Corp., lists Wilmington, Delaware as its corporate headquarters while maintaining a research and development office in Porto Alegre, Brazil, where the project originated; employee counts across data providers range roughly from 150 to just over 175 people. The platform's free, self-hosted Community edition supports up to fifty users and includes end-to-end encryption, unlimited message history, video conferencing integration, and data loss prevention controls, making it a popular choice for privacy-conscious teams, developers, and organizations that need to keep chat data within their own infrastructure. Paid tiers extend capacity and add enterprise features: the Pro plan is commonly cited at eight dollars per user per month on annual billing and supports up to five hundred users with advanced hosting options, white-label distribution rights, and standard support, while custom-quoted Enterprise, Government, and Defense plans add high availability architecture, advanced governance and audit trails, SCIM and SSO provisioning, FIPS 140-3 validated cryptography, federation capabilities, and air-gapped or SCIF-ready deployment options for regulated and public-sector customers. Rocket.Chat differentiates itself from many SaaS chat competitors by offering full source-code access and flexible deployment models spanning on-premises, private cloud, isolated VPC or VPN, and sovereign cloud environments, which has made it a common choice for government agencies, defense contractors, and organizations with strict data residency requirements. It is important not to confuse Rocket.Chat with the similarly-prefixed Rocket Matter, a completely unrelated legal practice management SaaS product for law firms; the two companies share no ownership, technology, or business relationship beyond the coincidental name similarity. Rocket.Chat's core feature set includes channel-based messaging, direct messages, voice and video calling, screen sharing, mobile apps for iOS and Android, omnichannel customer service tools, and an extensive apps marketplace and API for building custom integrations and bots, positioning it as both a team collaboration tool and a customer-facing communication platform depending on how an organization deploys it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rocket.Chat is an open-source team chat and collaboration platform founded in 2015, built as a self-hostable alternative to proprietary messaging tools like Slack and Microsoft Teams. The company, Rocket.Chat Technologies Corp., lists Wilmington, Delaware as its headquarters while maintaining an R&D office in Porto Alegre, Brazil, where the project began.

The platform is designed around flexibility of deployment and data control, offering both a free self-hosted Community edition and paid Pro, Enterprise, Government, and Defense plans aimed at organizations with stricter security and compliance requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rocket.Chat supports channel-based team messaging, direct messages, voice and video calls, screen sharing, and mobile apps for iOS and Android. It also includes omnichannel customer service tools, letting organizations use the same platform for internal collaboration and customer-facing chat.

Because Rocket.Chat is open source, teams can self-host it on-premises, in a private cloud, or in an isolated VPC or VPN, and higher tiers add federation, SCIM and SSO provisioning, FIPS 140-3 validated cryptography, and audit logging for regulated environments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The free Community edition supports up to fifty users with end-to-end encryption, unlimited message history, and video conferencing integration, self-hosted at no cost. The Pro plan is commonly priced around eight dollars per user per month on annual billing, extending capacity to five hundred users with white-label options and standard support.

Enterprise, Government, and Defense plans are custom-quoted and add high availability architecture, sovereign or air-gapped deployment options, and advanced governance controls for large organizations and public-sector customers.

features.description: Run Rocket.Chat on your own infrastructure, on-premises, in a private cloud, or in an isolated VPC or VPN for full data control.

features.description: Extend functionality with community and custom apps, bots, and integrations through an open API.

features.description: Connect multiple Rocket.Chat instances or federate with other servers for cross-organization communication.

features.title: Government and defense-grade deployments
features.description: FIPS 140-3 validated cryptography and air-gapped, SCIF-ready deployment options for regulated and public-sector customers.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps keep teams connected outside the desktop client.

pros: Free, self-hosted Community edition with no user-count-based licensing cost beyond infrastructure
Full source code access supports auditing and customization
Flexible deployment options including air-gapped and sovereign cloud for regulated industries
Combines internal team chat and customer-facing omnichannel support in one platform
cons: Self-hosting requires infrastructure management and technical expertise the hosted competitors don't demand
Enterprise, Government, and Defense pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Community edition caps out at fifty users before requiring a paid plan
UI and performance can lag behind polished proprietary competitors like Slack for casual users

faqs.answer: Yes, Rocket.Chat offers Government and Defense plans with air-gapped, SCIF-ready deployment and FIPS 140-3 validated cryptography.

enrichment.seo_title: Rollbar Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare Rollbar's error monitoring pricing tiers, features, and pros and cons before choosing an observability tool for your team.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rollbar is a cloud-native error monitoring and observability platform founded in 2012 by Brian Rue and Cory Virok and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Employee count estimates vary significantly across data providers, ranging from roughly 35 to 85 people depending on the source and collection date, which is typical for a company of its size and stage. Rollbar's core product automatically detects, groups, and alerts on errors, exceptions, and crash reports across an application's server, client, and mobile codebases, using intelligent error grouping to reduce noisy duplicate alerts and surface the underlying root cause rather than individual stack traces. The platform provides SDKs for a wide range of programming languages and frameworks, a REST API, and a Terraform provider for infrastructure-as-code users, and it supports real-time alerting, deploy tracking, and session replay to help engineering teams reproduce and fix issues faster. Pricing is structured around a free tier offering five thousand events per month, one thousand session replays per month, thirty-day data retention, and unlimited users and projects with no credit card required. Paid tiers scale along three dimensions: events (errors, messages, and crash reports sent to Rollbar's API), session replays, and AI credits used for AI-assisted triage and analysis features. The Essentials plan starts at twenty-nine dollars per month for fifty thousand events, offering up to fifty million events, ninety to one hundred eighty days of retention depending on volume, customizable rate limiting, and several thousand AI credits. The Advanced plan starts at one hundred twenty-nine dollars per month and adds continuous-delivery-oriented workflow features such as Adaptive Alerts, an enhanced dashboard with deploy markers, a cross-project error feed, team assignment routing, the Rollbar Query Language (RQL) and Metrics API for custom analytics, SAML single sign-on, and SCIM user provisioning. A custom-quoted Enterprise plan is available for organizations exceeding eighty million monthly events, offering fully custom data retention, priority support with SLAs, and dedicated success management. Annual billing across all paid plans saves roughly seventeen percent compared to monthly billing, equivalent to about two free months per year. Rollbar competes with other error monitoring and application observability tools and is commonly adopted by engineering teams that want detailed, code-level visibility into production errors without a general-purpose APM platform's full overhead.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rollbar is a cloud-native error monitoring and observability platform founded in 2012 by Brian Rue and Cory Virok, headquartered in San Francisco, California. It focuses specifically on detecting, grouping, and helping teams resolve application errors and exceptions in production and pre-production environments.
The platform is used across web, mobile, and server-side codebases, offering SDKs for a wide range of programming languages so teams can capture errors regardless of their tech stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rollbar's intelligent error grouping automatically clusters related occurrences of the same underlying issue, reducing alert noise and helping teams prioritize by real impact rather than raw event count. Real-time alerts and deploy tracking tie errors back to specific code changes, and session replay helps engineers see exactly what a user did before an error occurred.

Advanced plans add Adaptive Alerts, the Rollbar Query Language (RQL) and Metrics API for custom analytics, SAML SSO, and SCIM provisioning, positioning Rollbar for teams that need tighter integration with their existing identity and workflow tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rollbar's free plan includes five thousand events and one thousand session replays per month with thirty-day retention and no credit card required. Essentials starts at twenty-nine dollars per month, and Advanced starts at one hundred twenty-nine dollars per month, both scaling with event volume, session replays, and AI credits.

A custom-quoted Enterprise plan serves organizations with more than eighty million monthly events, adding fully custom retention and dedicated success management. Annual billing saves roughly seventeen percent versus paying monthly.

features.title: Intelligent error grouping
features.description: Automatically clusters related error occurrences into a single issue to cut down on alert noise and highlight true root causes.

features.title: Real-time alerts
features.description: Get notified immediately when new or reoccurring errors appear in production or other monitored environments.

features.title: Deploy tracking
features.description: Correlate error spikes with specific code deployments to quickly identify which release introduced a regression.

features.title: Session replay
features.description: Review what a user did leading up to an error to speed up reproduction and debugging.

features.title: Rollbar Query Language and Metrics API
features.description: Run custom queries and build metrics dashboards on error data for advanced analytics, available on Advanced and Enterprise plans.

features.title: Broad SDK support
features.description: Instrument applications across dozens of programming languages and frameworks with official SDKs.

features.title: SAML SSO and SCIM provisioning
features.description: Enterprise-grade identity integration for teams managing access at scale, available on Advanced and Enterprise tiers.

features.title: Terraform provider
features.description: Manage Rollbar configuration as infrastructure-as-code using the official Terraform provider.

pros: Generous free tier with no credit card required and unlimited users and projects
Intelligent error grouping reduces alert fatigue compared to raw exception logs
Broad language and framework SDK support
Advanced plans include SSO, SCIM, and custom query tooling for larger engineering teams
cons: Usage-based pricing across three dimensions (events, replays, AI credits) can make cost forecasting harder than flat per-seat pricing
Advanced analytics features like RQL are gated behind the higher-priced Advanced tier
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Session replay and AI credit allotments may require careful monitoring to avoid overage costs

faqs.question: What is Rollbar used for?
faqs.answer: Rollbar is used to detect, group, and alert on application errors and exceptions in real time across web, mobile, and server-side codebases.

faqs.question: Does Rollbar have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rollbar's free plan includes five thousand events and one thousand session replays per month with no credit card required.

faqs.question: How much does Rollbar cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at twenty-nine dollars per month for Essentials and one hundred twenty-nine dollars per month for Advanced, both scaling with usage.

faqs.question: When was Rollbar founded?
faqs.answer: Rollbar was founded in 2012 by Brian Rue and Cory Virok.

faqs.question: Where is Rollbar headquartered?
faqs.answer: Rollbar is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Does Rollbar support session replay?
faqs.answer: Yes, session replay is included on all plans, with the quota scaling from one thousand replays per month on the free tier upward on paid plans.

faqs.question: What is Rollbar's pricing based on?
faqs.answer: Rollbar's pricing scales along three dimensions: events sent to the API, session replays, and AI credits, rather than a flat per-seat fee.

faqs.question: Does Rollbar integrate with identity providers?
faqs.answer: Yes, SAML SSO and SCIM provisioning are available on the Advanced and Enterprise plans.

enrichment.short_description: RomM is a free, open-source, self-hosted ROM manager and player that lets users organize, enrich, and play retro video game collections from a web browser.
enrichment.seo_title: RomM Review: Self-Hosted Open-Source ROM Manager Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what RomM is, how its self-hosted ROM management features work, and what it takes to run this open-source retro gaming tool.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RomM, short for ROM Manager, is a free and open-source, self-hosted application for organizing, enriching, and playing retro video game ROM collections through a web browser, created by a developer known online as zurdi15 and maintained under the rommapp GitHub organization. The project is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPL-3.0) and has attracted a sizable open-source following, with its GitHub repository accumulating over 11,000 stars. RomM is designed for the self-hosting and homelab community, typically deployed via Docker on a home server or NAS alongside other self-hosted applications, and it explicitly positions itself against tracking, upsells, and shareholder-driven software, describing itself as built for its users rather than for commercial gain. The core functionality scans a user's existing ROM library and enriches it with metadata pulled from sources including IGDB, ScreenScraper, MobyGames, RetroAchievements, Hasheous, and LaunchBox, automatically fetching box art, descriptions, and platform information while correctly recognizing and grouping multi-file ROM sets, DLCs, mods, and manuals to avoid duplicate library entries. RomM supports metadata and organization across more than four hundred gaming platforms and includes in-browser emulation through EmulatorJS and Ruffle (for Flash content), meaning users can play many supported games directly in a web browser without installing separate emulator software. The platform includes a first-class multi-user system with configurable per-user permissions and OpenID Connect (OIDC) single sign-on support, letting a self-hoster share limited, controlled access to their library with friends or family while retaining full administrative control. Additional integrations include RetroAchievements support for earning achievements in classic games, and companion or community-built clients spanning an official Android app (Argosy), custom firmware handheld device support (Grout), and community projects covering iOS, RetroArch synchronization, desktop clients, SteamOS, and Nintendo Switch homebrew, as well as a Discord bot integration and an official Playnite plugin. Because RomM is a community open-source project rather than a commercial company, there is no pricing, corporate headquarters, or verified employee count to report, and its founding date is not clearly documented in public sources, though its GitHub activity and release history show active, ongoing development as of 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RomM is a free, open-source, self-hosted ROM manager and player for organizing and playing retro video game collections. Created by developer zurdi15 and maintained under the rommapp GitHub organization, it is licensed under AGPL-3.0 and has grown a substantial following in the self-hosting and homelab community, with its GitHub repository passing 11,000 stars.

The application is typically deployed via Docker on a home server or NAS, and it explicitly markets itself as a community-driven alternative built without tracking or upsells, in contrast to many commercial retro gaming or media server products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: RomM scans an existing ROM library and automatically enriches it with metadata, box art, and descriptions pulled from sources like IGDB, ScreenScraper, MobyGames, and LaunchBox, while correctly grouping multi-file ROM sets, DLCs, mods, and manuals to keep the library clean. It supports metadata for more than four hundred gaming platforms.

In-browser play is powered by EmulatorJS and Ruffle, letting users launch many supported games directly from a web browser. The platform also includes multi-user support with configurable permissions, OIDC single sign-on, and RetroAchievements integration for tracking in-game achievements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RomM is completely free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or commercial edition. Users self-host it on their own hardware, typically via Docker, and the only ongoing cost is the infrastructure they choose to run it on.

features.title: Automatic metadata enrichment
features.description: Pulls box art, descriptions, and platform data from IGDB, ScreenScraper, MobyGames, RetroAchievements, Hasheous, and LaunchBox.

features.title: Multi-file ROM grouping
features.description: Correctly recognizes and groups multi-disc or multi-file ROM sets, DLCs, mods, and manuals to avoid duplicate library entries.

features.title: In-browser emulation
features.description: Play supported games directly in a web browser using EmulatorJS and Ruffle without installing separate emulator software.

features.title: Multi-user support with permissions
features.description: Share a library with friends or family through configurable per-user permissions and OIDC single sign-on.

features.title: RetroAchievements integration
features.description: Connect RetroAchievements to earn and track achievements in classic games within RomM.

features.title: 400+ platform support
features.description: Organize and manage metadata across more than four hundred gaming platforms and consoles.

features.title: Companion apps and integrations
features.description: Official Android app, custom firmware handheld support, Playnite plugin, and community clients for iOS, RetroArch, SteamOS, and Nintendo Switch homebrew.

features.title: Self-hosted, no tracking
features.description: Runs entirely on the user's own infrastructure with no telemetry, upsells, or vendor lock-in.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Strong metadata enrichment from multiple respected sources in one place
In-browser emulation avoids needing separate emulator installations for many platforms
Active open-source community with over 11,000 GitHub stars and companion apps across platforms
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge, typically via Docker, with no managed hosting option
No official customer support channel beyond community resources
Legal use depends entirely on the user owning legitimate copies of any ROMs added to the library
Some companion apps and integrations are community-maintained rather than officially supported

faqs.question: What is RomM
faqs.answer: RomM is a free, open-source, self-hosted ROM manager and player for organizing and playing retro video game collections from a web browser.

faqs.question: Is RomM free
faqs.answer: Yes, RomM is completely free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host RomM
faqs.answer: Yes, RomM is a self-hosted application typically deployed via Docker on your own server or NAS; there is no official managed hosting service.

faqs.question: Can I play games directly in RomM
faqs.answer: Yes, RomM supports in-browser emulation for many platforms using EmulatorJS and Ruffle.

faqs.question: Where does RomM get game metadata and box art
faqs.answer: RomM pulls metadata from sources including IGDB, ScreenScraper, MobyGames, RetroAchievements, Hasheous, and LaunchBox.

faqs.question: Does RomM support multiple users
faqs.answer: Yes, RomM includes multi-user support with configurable permissions and OIDC single sign-on.

faqs.question: Is RomM legal to use
faqs.answer: RomM itself is legal open-source software; legality of use depends on whether the ROM files added to the library are legally owned by the user.

faqs.question: Does RomM have a mobile app
faqs.answer: Yes, there is an official Android app (Argosy), along with community-built clients for other platforms.

enrichment.short_description: RosarioSIS is a free, open-source student information system that helps K-12 schools manage students, grades, scheduling, attendance, and billing in one web application.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source, with optional paid add-ons and hosting services
languages: English, French, Spanish, Portuguese (Brazil), Italian, Russian, Ukrainian, Czech, Greek, Turkish, German, Vietnamese, Bulgarian
enrichment.seo_title: RosarioSIS Review: Free Open-Source Student Information System
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what RosarioSIS offers schools for free, from grading and scheduling to attendance and billing, plus its open-source licensing details.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RosarioSIS is a free, open-source student information system, also referred to as a school management system or school ERP, created by developer François Jacquet and released under the GNU General Public License version 2. The project traces its roots to 2012, when it was developed for Nuestra Señora del Rosario, a school in Colombia, building on an earlier source project called Centre/SIS that dates back to 2006. RosarioSIS is designed primarily for K-12 schools but is flexible enough to adapt to other educational institutions such as universities, academies, or vocational institutes. The platform bundles several modules into a single web application: student records and enrollment, a comprehensive grade book allowing teachers to define grade ranges, weight assignments, and track coursework toward quarterly and semester grades, report cards and transcripts, class and individual student scheduling, electronic attendance tracking that lets teachers submit records instantly to the school office, student billing for assigning fees and tracking payments and balances, as well as discipline and food service modules. The software has a responsive design that works on tablets and smartphones and has been translated by its community into a substantial number of languages, including French, Spanish, Portuguese (Brazil), Italian, Russian, Ukrainian, Czech, Greek, Turkish, German, Vietnamese, and Bulgarian, in addition to its default English interface, making it accessible to schools well beyond English-speaking countries. It also integrates with the Moodle learning management system and offers numerous free and premium add-ons for schools that want to extend the core functionality. Technically, RosarioSIS requires a PHP runtime of at least version 5.5.9 and either PostgreSQL 9.2 or MySQL 5.6 / MariaDB as its database backend, and schools are expected to self-host the application on their own or a third-party server since there is no official managed cloud version. The project's stated philosophy emphasizes that RosarioSIS is free and will always remain so, framing this as a matter of giving schools strategic autonomy and digital sovereignty over their student data rather than depending on a proprietary vendor. Because it is a community-driven open-source project rather than a venture-backed company, there is no formal pricing structure, corporate headquarters, or publicly reported employee headcount; support is provided through community forums, documentation, and the project's GitHub and GitLab repositories.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RosarioSIS is a free, open-source student information system created by developer François Jacquet, originally built in 2012 for a school in Colombia and released under the GNU General Public License version 2. It targets K-12 schools but can be adapted for other types of educational institutions.

The application bundles student records, grading, scheduling, attendance, billing, discipline, and food service into a single self-hosted web application, with a responsive design that works across desktops, tablets, and smartphones.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: RosarioSIS's grade book lets teachers define grade ranges and assignment weights and produces report cards and transcripts, while its scheduler supports both class-wide and individual student scheduling with quick revision tools. Attendance can be recorded electronically by teachers and adjusted by office staff as needed.

The billing module tracks fees, payments, and balances, and the platform integrates with the Moodle learning management system. It has been translated into more than a dozen languages by its community and supports numerous free and premium add-ons.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RosarioSIS is free and open source under the GPL v2 license, with the project's stated philosophy that it will always remain free to give schools strategic autonomy over their data. Optional premium add-ons and third-party hosting services are available for schools that want extended functionality or managed infrastructure.

features.title: Student records and enrollment
features.description: Centralized management of student information, enrollment status, and demographic data.

features.title: Comprehensive grade book
features.description: Teachers can set grade ranges, weight assignments, and generate quarterly and semester grades, report cards, and transcripts.

features.description: Schedule entire classes or individual students, with quick tools for revising a student's schedule.

features.title: Electronic attendance
features.description: Teachers record attendance electronically, with office staff able to review and adjust submitted records.

features.title: Student billing
features.description: Assign fees, track payments and balances, and print billing statements for families.

features.title: Discipline and food service modules
features.description: Additional modules for tracking student discipline records and managing school food service.

features.title: Moodle integration
features.description: Built-in integration with the Moodle learning management system.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Translated into a dozen-plus languages by the community, in addition to the default English interface.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPL v2 with no license fees
Covers a wide range of school administration modules in one application
Extensive community translations make it accessible to non-English-speaking schools
Responsive design works on tablets and smartphones as well as desktops
cons: Requires self-hosting and technical setup with PHP and PostgreSQL or MySQL/MariaDB
No official managed cloud hosting from the core project
Some advanced functionality is only available through premium add-ons
Support relies on community forums and documentation rather than dedicated commercial support

faqs.question: Is RosarioSIS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, RosarioSIS is free and open source under the GNU General Public License version 2.

faqs.question: Who created RosarioSIS?
faqs.answer: RosarioSIS was created by developer François Jacquet, originally built in 2012 for a school in Colombia.

faqs.question: What schools is RosarioSIS designed for?
faqs.answer: It is primarily designed for K-12 schools but can adapt to universities, academies, and other educational institutions.

faqs.question: What languages is RosarioSIS available in?
faqs.answer: RosarioSIS is available in English plus more than a dozen community-translated languages including French, Spanish, Italian, and German.

faqs.question: Does RosarioSIS require self-hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, schools self-host RosarioSIS on their own or third-party servers; there is no official managed cloud version.

faqs.question: What are the technical requirements for RosarioSIS?
faqs.answer: RosarioSIS requires PHP 5.5.9 or later and either PostgreSQL 9.2 or MySQL 5.6 / MariaDB as the database.

faqs.question: Does RosarioSIS integrate with Moodle?
faqs.answer: Yes, RosarioSIS includes integration with the Moodle learning management system.

faqs.question: Does RosarioSIS have paid add-ons?
faqs.answer: Yes, in addition to the free core system, RosarioSIS offers numerous free and premium add-ons for extended functionality.

enrichment.short_description: Rosegarden is a free, open-source audio and MIDI sequencer with a full music notation editor, built for Linux.
enrichment.seo_title: Rosegarden Review: Free Open-Source MIDI Sequencer for Linux
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what Rosegarden offers musicians on Linux, from MIDI sequencing to notation editing, and how this open-source project got started.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rosegarden is a free, open-source music composition and editing environment for Linux that combines a track-oriented MIDI and audio sequencer with a full music notation editor. Its roots trace back to 1993, when it began as an academic project at the University of Bath started by Chris Cannam and Andy Green; a Linux port followed in 1995 with developer Richard Bown joining, and Guillaume Laurent joined in 1996 during its early C/Athena-based phase. The current incarnation of the project, which shares its name and general concept with the original but is a fresh codebase, restarted at the beginning of 2000 with a new SourceForge presence, and Cannam, Bown, and Laurent led development through roughly 2000 to 2003 up to the 1.0 release. Leadership passed to D. Michael McIntyre from around 2003 to 2008, who continued as a primary maintainer through 2017, and as of 2023 Ted Felix has led ongoing development and releases. Rosegarden is licensed under the GNU General Public License, allowing free redistribution and modification, and the project is aimed at composers, musicians, music students, and small studio or home recording setups looking for an integrated, no-cost alternative to commercial digital audio workstation and notation software. Distinctively, Rosegarden treats musical notation as a first-class citizen rather than a simple MIDI-to-notation conversion layer: it stores detailed information about musical structure and presentation that goes beyond what standard MIDI data alone can represent, enabling higher-quality printed notation output than most conventional MIDI sequencers can produce. On the audio and MIDI side, it supports the JACK audio server for professional audio routing, LADSPA and DSSI plugin formats for synthesis and audio processing, and advanced ALSA MIDI features including multiplexed input/output and synchronization with external hardware and software via MIDI Clock, MIDI Machine Control (MMC), and MIDI Time Code (MTC). The software also automatically assigns MIDI channels to simplify working with multiple instruments. As a long-running community open-source project rather than a commercial company, Rosegarden has no pricing tiers, corporate structure, headquarters, or reported employee count; it is distributed through SourceForge, Codeberg, and GitHub mirrors, and users typically install it via their Linux distribution's package manager or build it from source.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rosegarden is a free, open-source audio and MIDI sequencer with a full music notation editor, built specifically for Linux. Its origins trace back to 1993 as an academic project at the University of Bath started by Chris Cannam and Andy Green, with the current codebase restarting fresh in 2000 under a new SourceForge project.

Development has passed through several lead maintainers over the decades, from the original core team of Cannam, Richard Bown, and Guillaume Laurent, through D. Michael McIntyre in the 2000s and 2010s, to Ted Felix leading the project as of 2023.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rosegarden combines a track-oriented MIDI and audio sequencer with a notation editor that treats musical structure and presentation as first-class data, producing higher-quality printed notation than typical MIDI-only sequencers. It supports the JACK audio server, LADSPA and DSSI plugins for synthesis and processing, and advanced ALSA MIDI features.

The software supports synchronization with external hardware and software through MIDI Clock, MIDI Machine Control, and MIDI Time Code, along with automatic MIDI channel assignment to simplify multi-instrument setups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rosegarden is free software distributed under the GNU General Public License, with no cost to download, use, or modify. It is typically installed through a Linux distribution's package manager or built from source.

features.title: Combined MIDI and audio sequencing

features.title: Full music notation editor

features.title: Advanced ALSA MIDI features
features.description: Supports multiplexed MIDI input/output and synchronization with external devices via MIDI Clock, MMC, and MTC.

features.title: Automatic MIDI channel assignment
features.description: Simplifies working with multiple instruments by automatically assigning MIDI channels.

features.title: Free and open source
features.description: Distributed under the GNU General Public License with full source code access and no licensing cost.

features.title: Long-running active development
features.description: Continuously maintained since a 2000 codebase restart, with active releases as of 2023 under current lead Ted Felix.

pros: Completely free and open source under the GPL
Rare combination of serious notation editing and MIDI/audio sequencing in one application
Supports professional Linux audio tooling like JACK, LADSPA, and DSSI
Long project history spanning three decades with continued active maintenance
cons: Linux-only, with no native Windows or macOS builds
Interface and workflow feel dated compared to modern commercial DAWs
Audio recording and editing capabilities are more basic than dedicated DAW software
Smaller community and slower update cadence than mainstream commercial alternatives

faqs.question: Is Rosegarden free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rosegarden is free and open-source software distributed under the GNU General Public License.

faqs.question: What platforms does Rosegarden run on?
faqs.answer: Rosegarden is developed specifically for Linux, using ALSA and JACK for audio and MIDI.

faqs.question: When did Rosegarden start?
faqs.answer: Rosegarden began as an academic project in 1993 at the University of Bath; the current codebase restarted fresh in 2000.

faqs.question: Who currently maintains Rosegarden?
faqs.answer: As of 2023, Ted Felix has been leading Rosegarden's development and releases.

faqs.question: Does Rosegarden support music notation, not just MIDI?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rosegarden includes a full notation editor that stores musical structure and presentation data beyond what standard MIDI can represent.

faqs.question: Does Rosegarden support audio plugins?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports LADSPA and DSSI plugin formats for synthesis and audio processing.

faqs.question: Can Rosegarden sync with external hardware?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports MIDI Clock, MIDI Machine Control, and MIDI Time Code for synchronization with external devices and software.

faqs.question: Where can I download Rosegarden?
faqs.answer: Rosegarden is distributed through SourceForge, Codeberg, and GitHub mirrors, and is often available via Linux distribution package managers.

use_cases.title: Linux audio production with JACK
use_cases.description: An audio hobbyist integrates Rosegarden with JACK, LADSPA, and DSSI plugins as part of a broader Linux audio production setup.
